title: COMPAT — Constraint-Market Compatibility: Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing (NAICS 333318)

sections.title: Step 07 — Enriched Product Constraints
sections.content:
12 constraints extracted from upstream inputs (8 from Step 01, 4 added during enrichment). 3 absolute, 9 conditional.

| # | Constraint | Type | Threshold | Unit | Absolute | Affected Markets Hint |
|---|-----------|------|-----------|------|----------|----------------------|
| 1 | Media Limitation | physical | — | — | **yes** | Petrochemical, Plastics, Gas Metering, Lubricant Processing |
| 2 | Maximum Medium Temperature | physical | 90 | °C | **yes** | Steelmaking, Glass Mfg, Steam Systems, Chemical Processing |
| 3 | Maximum Operating Pressure | physical | 10 | bar | no | High-Pressure Chemical, Power Generation, Oil & Gas |
| 4 | Air Bubble Tolerance | physical | 2 | % vol | no | Aerated Beverages, Pulp & Paper, Fermentation Off-Gas |
| 5 | Temperature-Dependent Accuracy | physical | 1.5 (uncompensated) | % error | no | Wide-Delta-T Processes, CIP Systems |
| 6 | Detergent/Chemical Compatibility | chemical | varies | — | no | Commercial Laundry, Car Wash, Industrial Cleaning |
| 7 | Minimum Flow Rate Detection | operational | 0.25–4.2 | l/min | no | Low-Flow Lab, Micro-Dosing, Drip Irrigation |
| 8 | Pipe Full Condition | operational | — | — | **yes** | Open-Channel, Gravity Drain, Partially Filled Sewers |
| 9 | Minimum Straight Pipe Run | operational | 5–10 | × DN | no | Compact Installations, Retrofit, OEM Space-Constrained Equipment |
| 10 | Unit Cost vs Mechanical Alternatives | economic | 200–600 | USD | no | Low-Margin Commodity, Small Craft Operations |
| 11 | NSF/Food-Contact Material Certification | regulatory | NSF 61 / FDA 21 CFR 177 | — | no | Beverage, Potable Water, Ice, Food Processing |
| 12 | Ambient Operating Temperature | environmental | −20 to +60 | °C | no | Cold Storage, Desert Outdoor, Foundry Proximity |

**Type distribution:** 5 physical, 1 chemical, 3 operational, 1 economic, 1 regulatory, 1 environmental — all 6 types represented.

---

sections.title: Step 08 — Constraint-Market Assessment: Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing (333318)
sections.content:
Marquardt positions in the commercial machinery value network [SRC: VN_other_commercial_and_service_industry_ma]:
- **Primary:** Water supply & distribution circuits (L6.2) — metering fill volume, consumption, and flow anomalies in washer-extractors, car wash systems, and water purification equipment (clean water / dilute detergent, 10–40 °C typical, 3–6 bar)
- **Secondary:** Reclaim/recycling circuits (L6.6) — measuring reclaimed water reuse volumes for water-efficiency reporting and WaterSense compliance
- **Tertiary:** Effluent monitoring (L6.6) — total discharge volume metering for regulatory water-balance reporting

### Assessment Matrix

| # | Constraint | Verdict | Rationale |
|---|-----------|---------|-----------|
| 1 | Media Limitation | **none** | All circuits use water or water-based solutions. Commercial laundry: water + dilute detergent/surfactant. Car wash: water + dilute soap/chemical. Water purification: clean water. No oils, gases, or non-polar liquids in measurement circuits. [SRC: 01, VN] |
| 2 | Max Medium Temp (90 °C) | **mitigable** | Commercial laundry sanitary/heavy-soil wash cycles reach 85–90 °C, exactly at the sensor limit. Car wash pre-soak/rinse: 30–60 °C (well within). Water treatment: ambient. Extended operation at 90 °C reduces sensor service life. Majority of commercial wash cycles operate at 40–60 °C. |
|   |  | | **Mitigation:** (1) Install sensor on water supply inlet or return lines (5–15 °C cooler than wash sump); (2) Prioritize car wash and water treatment positions (max 60 °C); (3) For laundry, target warm-wash circuits and position upstream of heating element. |
|   |  | | **Cost:** low (<$10K) — **Time:** design integration (1–2 months) |
| 3 | Max Pressure (10 bar) | **none** | Building water supply 3–6 bar. Laundry circuits 2–5 bar. Car wash HP pumps generate 50–150 bar at nozzle, but sensor sits on supply side (3–6 bar), NOT downstream of HP pump. Water treatment feed pressure 2–8 bar. No measurement circuit approaches 10 bar. [SRC: VN] |
| 4 | Air Bubble Tolerance (2% vol) | **none** | Sensor positioned in pressurized supply-side piping (L6.2) where chronic air entrainment is negligible. Laundry drum fill occurs via pressurized inlet — air enters drum, not supply pipe. Car wash supply circuits are closed and pressurized. Sensor's digital anomaly detection provides diagnostic value if transient air enters. [SRC: 01, VN] |
| 5 | Temp-Dependent Accuracy | **none** | Supply-side water temperature is stable during fill/wash cycles. Built-in NTC compensation reduces error to ~0.2%, adequate for consumption metering and dose verification. Temperature transitions during cycle changes are gradual. [SRC: 01] |
| 6 | Detergent/Chemical Compatibility | **mitigable** | In some circuit configurations, sensor wetted parts may contact detergent-laden wash water (laundry recirculation), surfactant solutions (car wash), or anti-scalant-dosed water (RO treatment). Concentrations are dilute (0.5–2% detergent, pH 8–12) — less aggressive than brewery CIP (2–3% NaOH at 85 °C). Primary entry position (supply-side metering) contacts clean water only. |
|   |  | | **Mitigation:** (1) Position sensor on supply side upstream of chemical injection (L6.2 before L6.4) for clean-water-only contact; (2) Verify polymer housing compatibility with dilute alkaline detergent (pH 10–12) via immersion testing; (3) For reclaim circuit deployment, validate against recycled-water chemistry. |
|   |  | | **Cost:** low (<$10K) — **Time:** 2–4 months |
| 7 | Min Flow Rate (0.25–4.2 l/min) | **none** | Commercial laundry fill: 10–40 l/min. Car wash supply: 15–40 l/min. Water purification feed: 5–20 l/min. All well above DN20 threshold (4.2 l/min). DN14 variant (0.25 l/min) available for lower-flow sub-circuits. [SRC: VN] |
| 8 | Pipe Full Condition | **none** | Supply-side piping is pressurized and always full during measurement. Machine controllers trigger measurement during active fill/wash/rinse cycles. Sensor's empty-pipe detection provides fault-state diagnostic during transitions. Drain-side deployment (L6.6) requires vertical or siphon installation to ensure full pipe. [SRC: 01, VN] |
| 9 | Min Straight Pipe Run (5–10× DN) | **mitigable** | Commercial machinery is designed for space efficiency. OEM internal plumbing may not have 5–10× DN (100–250 mm for DN20) straight runs at optimal sensor locations. Compact washer-extractors and in-bay car wash systems are especially constrained. |
|   |  | | **Mitigation:** (1) Specify sensor position during OEM machine design phase (not retrofit); (2) Add inline vane-type flow conditioner ($50–100) at space-constrained locations; (3) Provide OEM integration guidelines with minimum installation clearances. |
|   |  | | **Cost:** low (<$10K) — **Time:** design integration (1–2 months) |
| 10 | Unit Cost vs Mechanical | **none** | Ultrasonic ($200–600 est.) vs turbine/paddle-wheel ($5–15) carries a 15–40× price premium. However, turbine sensors clog in lint-laden laundry water and detergent-rich car wash water — the dominant failure mode in this market. Timed solenoid dosing (zero sensor cost) has ±10–20% accuracy, insufficient for water-efficiency reporting. Zero-maintenance ultrasonic justifies premium in high-duty commercial equipment (100+ cycles/day). ROI via eliminated maintenance and WaterSense compliance. [SRC: 01, VN] |
| 11 | NSF/Food-Contact Certification | **mitigable** | VN output types include ice machines and beverage vending — food-contact applications requiring NSF 61 or FDA 21 CFR 177 certification. Primary entry positions (commercial laundry, car wash, water treatment) do NOT require food-contact certification. KTW/WRAS drinking water approval provides partial baseline for potable water treatment applications. |
|   |  | | **Mitigation:** (1) Enter market via laundry and car wash positions (no food-contact, no certification needed); (2) Pursue NSF 61 certification to unlock water purification and ice/beverage positions; (3) Leverage existing KTW/WRAS approval as certification starting point. |
|   |  | | **Cost:** medium ($10K–100K) — **Time:** 6–12 months |
| 12 | Ambient Operating Temp | **none** | Commercial laundry: indoor facility (15–35 °C). Car wash: enclosed/sheltered bays (5–40 °C). Water treatment: indoor equipment room (15–30 °C). All well within electronics operating range (−20 to +60 °C). Outdoor self-service car wash in extreme climates is an edge case — machines themselves require freeze protection in those environments. [SRC: VN] |

### Verdict Summary

| Verdict | Count | Constraints |
|---------|-------|-------------|
| **knockout** | 0 | — |
| **mitigable** | 4 | Max Medium Temperature, Detergent/Chemical Compatibility, Min Straight Pipe Run, NSF/Food-Contact Certification |
| **none** | 8 | Media, Max Pressure, Air Bubbles, Temp Accuracy, Min Flow, Pipe Full, Unit Cost, Ambient Temp |

### Conservative Review (08b)

No knockouts to review. All 4 mitigable verdicts confirmed:
- Max medium temperature: sanitary laundry wash at 85–90 °C is at the sensor's rated limit — genuine concern for service life. Mitigable by positioning on supply side (cooler) and prioritizing car wash/water treatment entry.
- Detergent/chemical: dilute alkaline detergent exposure is a real but mild chemical concern. Primary supply-side position avoids contact entirely.
- Straight pipe run: compact OEM equipment is genuinely space-constrained. Standard solution (flow conditioner + design-phase integration) exists.
- NSF/food-contact: regulatory gap for ice/beverage output types. Bypassable by entering via non-food-contact positions first.

No verdict changes after review.

---

sections.title: Result
sections.content:
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| **Market** | Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing (NAICS 333318) |
| **Status** | **SURVIVING** |
| **Knockouts** | 0 |
| **Mitigable** | 4 (low: 3, medium: 1) |
| **None** | 8 |
| **markets_eliminated** | [] |
| **markets_surviving** | ["333318"] |

**Entry strategy implication:** All 4 mitigable constraints can be bypassed by entering via commercial laundry (warm wash only, supply-side metering) or car wash (30–60 °C, clean water circuits). These positions require zero certification changes and operate well within every constraint threshold. Water purification and ice/beverage positions can be unlocked incrementally after NSF 61 certification. The dominant value proposition — zero-maintenance, no-clog measurement in lint/detergent-laden environments — directly addresses this market's primary pain point with incumbent turbine sensors.

---

sections.title: Sources
sections.content:
- 01_product_decomposition.md — Constraints, specifications, features, technology classification
- 02_functional_promise.md — Functional promise, complements, BOM position
- 05_market_discovery.md — Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing (NAICS 333318) market candidate details
- VN_other_commercial_and_service_industry_ma.md — Value network, Marquardt position, operating conditions, output types

---

*End of COMPAT — Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing (333318) passes all constraints with 0 knockouts. Four mitigable barriers exist, all bypassable by entering via commercial laundry (warm-wash, supply-side) or car wash (30-60C, clean-water circuits) positions. The no-clog, zero-maintenance value proposition directly addresses this market's primary incumbent failure mode (turbine sensor clogging in lint/detergent water). Feeds downstream into Feature-Market Fit, Competitive Landscape, and Market Prioritization.*

rows.Affected Markets Hint: Petrochemical, Plastics, Gas Metering, Lubricant Processing
Steelmaking, Glass Mfg, Steam Systems, Chemical Processing
High-Pressure Chemical, Power Generation, Oil & Gas
Aerated Beverages, Pulp & Paper, Fermentation Off-Gas
Commercial Laundry, Car Wash, Industrial Cleaning
Open-Channel, Gravity Drain, Partially Filled Sewers
Compact Installations, Retrofit, OEM Space-Constrained Equipment
Beverage, Potable Water, Ice, Food Processing
Cold Storage, Desert Outdoor, Foundry Proximity

rows.Rationale: All circuits use water or water-based solutions. Commercial laundry: water + dilute detergent/surfactant. Car wash: water + dilute soap/chemical. Water purification: clean water. No oils, gases, or non-polar liquids in measurement circuits. [SRC: 01, VN]

Commercial laundry sanitary/heavy-soil wash cycles reach 85–90 °C, exactly at the sensor limit. Car wash pre-soak/rinse: 30–60 °C (well within). Water treatment: ambient. Extended operation at 90 °C reduces sensor service life. Majority of commercial wash cycles operate at 40–60 °C.

rows.#: **Mitigation:** (1) Install sensor on water supply inlet or return lines (5–15 °C cooler than wash sump); (2) Prioritize car wash and water treatment positions (max 60 °C); (3) For laundry, target warm-wash circuits and position upstream of heating element.

rows.Rationale: Building water supply 3–6 bar. Laundry circuits 2–5 bar. Car wash HP pumps generate 50–150 bar at nozzle, but sensor sits on supply side (3–6 bar), NOT downstream of HP pump. Water treatment feed pressure 2–8 bar. No measurement circuit approaches 10 bar. [SRC: VN]

Sensor positioned in pressurized supply-side piping (L6.2) where chronic air entrainment is negligible. Laundry drum fill occurs via pressurized inlet — air enters drum, not supply pipe. Car wash supply circuits are closed and pressurized. Sensor's digital anomaly detection provides diagnostic value if transient air enters. [SRC: 01, VN]

Supply-side water temperature is stable during fill/wash cycles. Built-in NTC compensation reduces error to ~0.2%, adequate for consumption metering and dose verification. Temperature transitions during cycle changes are gradual. [SRC: 01]

In some circuit configurations, sensor wetted parts may contact detergent-laden wash water (laundry recirculation), surfactant solutions (car wash), or anti-scalant-dosed water (RO treatment). Concentrations are dilute (0.5–2% detergent, pH 8–12) — less aggressive than brewery CIP (2–3% NaOH at 85 °C). Primary entry position (supply-side metering) contacts clean water only.

rows.#: **Mitigation:** (1) Position sensor on supply side upstream of chemical injection (L6.2 before L6.4) for clean-water-only contact; (2) Verify polymer housing compatibility with dilute alkaline detergent (pH 10–12) via immersion testing; (3) For reclaim circuit deployment, validate against recycled-water chemistry.

rows.Rationale: Commercial laundry fill: 10–40 l/min. Car wash supply: 15–40 l/min. Water purification feed: 5–20 l/min. All well above DN20 threshold (4.2 l/min). DN14 variant (0.25 l/min) available for lower-flow sub-circuits. [SRC: VN]

Supply-side piping is pressurized and always full during measurement. Machine controllers trigger measurement during active fill/wash/rinse cycles. Sensor's empty-pipe detection provides fault-state diagnostic during transitions. Drain-side deployment (L6.6) requires vertical or siphon installation to ensure full pipe. [SRC: 01, VN]

Commercial machinery is designed for space efficiency. OEM internal plumbing may not have 5–10× DN (100–250 mm for DN20) straight runs at optimal sensor locations. Compact washer-extractors and in-bay car wash systems are especially constrained.

rows.#: **Mitigation:** (1) Specify sensor position during OEM machine design phase (not retrofit); (2) Add inline vane-type flow conditioner ($50–100) at space-constrained locations; (3) Provide OEM integration guidelines with minimum installation clearances.

rows.Rationale: Ultrasonic ($200–600 est.) vs turbine/paddle-wheel ($5–15) carries a 15–40× price premium. However, turbine sensors clog in lint-laden laundry water and detergent-rich car wash water — the dominant failure mode in this market. Timed solenoid dosing (zero sensor cost) has ±10–20% accuracy, insufficient for water-efficiency reporting. Zero-maintenance ultrasonic justifies premium in high-duty commercial equipment (100+ cycles/day). ROI via eliminated maintenance and WaterSense compliance. [SRC: 01, VN]

VN output types include ice machines and beverage vending — food-contact applications requiring NSF 61 or FDA 21 CFR 177 certification. Primary entry positions (commercial laundry, car wash, water treatment) do NOT require food-contact certification. KTW/WRAS drinking water approval provides partial baseline for potable water treatment applications.

rows.#: **Mitigation:** (1) Enter market via laundry and car wash positions (no food-contact, no certification needed); (2) Pursue NSF 61 certification to unlock water purification and ice/beverage positions; (3) Leverage existing KTW/WRAS approval as certification starting point.

rows.Rationale: Commercial laundry: indoor facility (15–35 °C). Car wash: enclosed/sheltered bays (5–40 °C). Water treatment: indoor equipment room (15–30 °C). All well within electronics operating range (−20 to +60 °C). Outdoor self-service car wash in extreme climates is an edge case — machines themselves require freeze protection in those environments. [SRC: VN]

rows.Constraints: Max Medium Temperature, Detergent/Chemical Compatibility, Min Straight Pipe Run, NSF/Food-Contact Certification
Media, Max Pressure, Air Bubbles, Temp Accuracy, Min Flow, Pipe Full, Unit Cost, Ambient Temp

rows.Value: Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing (NAICS 333318)

entities.market_name: Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing

assessments.market_name: Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing
assessments.rationale: All circuits use water or water-based solutions. Commercial laundry: water + dilute detergent. Car wash: water + dilute soap. Water purification: clean water. No oils, gases, or non-polar liquids in measurement circuits.

assessments.market_name: Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing
mitigation_actions: Install sensor on water supply inlet or return lines (5-15C cooler than wash sump)
Prioritize car wash and water treatment positions (max 60C, well within limit)
For laundry, target warm-wash circuits and position upstream of heating element
assessments.rationale: Commercial laundry sanitary/heavy-soil wash cycles reach 85-90C, exactly at the sensor limit. Car wash 30-60C and water treatment ambient are well within range. Extended operation at 90C reduces service life. Majority of wash cycles operate at 40-60C.

assessments.market_name: Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing
assessments.rationale: Building water supply 3-6 bar. Laundry circuits 2-5 bar. Car wash HP pumps generate 50-150 bar at nozzle, but sensor sits on supply side (3-6 bar). Water treatment feed 2-8 bar. No measurement circuit approaches 10 bar.

assessments.market_name: Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing
assessments.rationale: Sensor positioned in pressurized supply-side piping (L6.2) where chronic air entrainment is negligible. Laundry fill occurs via pressurized inlet. Car wash supply circuits are closed and pressurized. Digital anomaly detection provides diagnostic value for transient events.

assessments.market_name: Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing
assessments.rationale: Supply-side water temperature is stable during fill/wash cycles. Built-in NTC compensation reduces error to 0.2%, adequate for consumption metering and dose verification.

assessments.market_name: Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing
mitigation_actions: Position sensor on supply side upstream of chemical injection (L6.2 before L6.4) for clean-water-only contact
Verify polymer housing compatibility with dilute alkaline detergent (pH 10-12) via immersion testing
For reclaim circuit deployment, validate sensor materials against recycled-water chemistry
assessments.rationale: Some circuit configurations expose wetted parts to detergent-laden wash water (laundry recirculation), surfactant solutions (car wash), or anti-scalant-dosed water (RO). Concentrations are dilute (0.5-2% detergent, pH 8-12). Primary supply-side position contacts clean water only.

assessments.market_name: Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing
assessments.rationale: Commercial laundry fill: 10-40 l/min. Car wash supply: 15-40 l/min. Water purification feed: 5-20 l/min. All above DN20 threshold (4.2 l/min). DN14 variant available for lower-flow sub-circuits.

assessments.market_name: Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing
assessments.rationale: Supply-side piping is pressurized and always full during measurement. Machine controllers trigger measurement during active fill/wash/rinse cycles. Empty-pipe detection provides fault-state diagnostic during transitions.

assessments.market_name: Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing
mitigation_actions: Specify sensor position during OEM machine design phase (not retrofit)
Add inline vane-type flow conditioner ($50-100) at space-constrained locations
Provide OEM integration guidelines with minimum installation clearances
assessments.rationale: Commercial machinery is designed for space efficiency. OEM internal plumbing may not have 5-10x DN (100-250 mm for DN20) straight runs. Compact washer-extractors and in-bay car wash systems especially constrained. Solvable during OEM design-in.

assessments.market_name: Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing
assessments.rationale: Ultrasonic ($200-600) vs turbine ($5-15) carries 15-40x premium. However, turbine sensors clog in lint-laden laundry water and detergent-rich car wash water — the dominant failure mode. Timed solenoid dosing has +/-10-20% accuracy, insufficient for WaterSense compliance. Zero-maintenance ROI in 100+ cycles/day equipment.

assessments.market_name: Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing
mitigation_actions: Enter market via laundry and car wash positions (no food-contact certification needed)
Pursue NSF 61 certification to unlock water purification and ice/beverage output types
Leverage existing KTW/WRAS drinking water approval as certification starting point
assessments.rationale: VN output types include ice machines and beverage vending — food-contact applications requiring NSF 61 or FDA 21 CFR 177. Primary entry positions (laundry, car wash) do NOT require food-contact certification. KTW/WRAS provides partial baseline.

assessments.market_name: Other Commercial and Service Industry Machinery Manufacturing
assessments.rationale: Commercial laundry: indoor (15-35C). Car wash: enclosed/sheltered bays (5-40C). Water treatment: indoor (15-30C). All within electronics range (-20 to +60C). Outdoor self-service car wash is edge case; machines themselves require freeze protection.
